result.response_content_preview:
THOUGHT:
I currently only have Sculley et al. (2015), which effectively covers engineering debt and infrastructure pitfalls. However, I am missing foundational literature on core algorithmic and statistical failure modes that plague deep learning in production, specifically **distribution shift and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization**. This is arguably the most critical technical challenge when moving from controlled benchmarks to real-world deployment. I need a citation that formally defines dataset shift as a failure concept (Category 2), provides empirical comparisons between benchmark and wild performance (Category 4), highlights the gap in current generalization theory (Category 5), and introduces established evaluation methodologies and datasets (Categories 3 & 6). The WILDS benchmark paper by Koh et al. is ideal for this purpose as it systematically documents real-world degradation across multiple domains, directly supporting arguments about the fragility of standard deep
